title: Zuckerberg says AI agent development going slower than expected
titleZh: 扎克伯格称AI智能体开发进展不及预期
author: cwwc
relevanceReason: 该帖子讨论的是扎克伯格关于AI代理开发进展慢于预期的言论，直接涉及AI技术发展动态，属于AI领域的重要行业新闻。
summaryZh: 扎克伯格表示AI代理开发进展慢于预期
Meta在AI代理技术路线上面临挑战
highlightsZh: AI代理技术发展速度不及行业预期
Meta CEO公开承认AI代理开发遇到瓶颈
引发对当前AI技术发展节奏的讨论
可能影响AI代理产品的商业化进程

comments.author: efficax
comments.textRawHtml: I was worried this time last year that by this time this year, companies would have slashed their engineering teams down to a handful and everything would be driven by mostly autonomous agents with human guidance. But it just hasn't happened. Do I write all my code with an agent now? Yes. Can you just give an agent a desired outcome and let it work, unsupervised? Absolutely not. I can produce more code than I used to, but if I want it to be good, to be stable, to do what the product manager and designers want, it's only about 2 to 3 times more code than before. And that productivity is impacted by the fact that I'm reviewing 2 to 3 times more code than before (and you have to review, even more so now than before, because if you just let opus or gpt 5 do its thing, you'll get some terrible results, and I've found a lot of engineers on my team are just letting it do it's thing without a lot of iteration).
comments.textZhHtml: 去年的这个时候我很担心，到今年这个时候，公司可能会将其工程团队削减到少数人，一切都将由大多数自主代理在人工指导下驱动。但这还没有发生。我现在需要通过代理编写所有代码吗？是的。您能否为代理提供所需的结果并让它在无人监督的情况下发挥作用？绝对不是。我可以编写比以前更多的代码，但如果我想让它变得好、稳定、做产品经理和设计师想要的事情，那么只需要比以前多大约 2 到 3 倍的代码。我审查的代码比以前多了 2 到 3 倍，这影响了我的工作效率（而且你必须要审查，现在比以前还要多，因为如果你只是让 opus 或 gpt 5 做它的事情，你会得到一些糟糕的结果，而且我发现我团队中的很多工程师只是让它做它的事情而不进行大量迭代）。

children.author: andrewaylett
children.textRawHtml:
The thing is: I could produce 2-3 times as much code as before _without_ an LLM, if I didn't care about my colleagues' ability to review my output properly.

Lines of code are a liability, not an asset. You want as few of them as you can get away with, without compromising the actual asset: the functionality.

A huge part of the job of Software Engineering is producing the right amount of code at the right time.
children.textZhHtml:
问题是：如果我不关心同事正确审查我的输出的能力，那么在没有 LLM 的情况下，我可以编写出比以前多 2-3 倍的代码。

代码行是一种负担，而不是一种资产。 您希望在不影响实际资产（功能）的情况下使用尽可能少的代码。

软件工程工作的一个重要部分是在正确的时间生成正确数量的代码。

children.author: ben_w
children.textRawHtml:
> Lines of code are a liability, not an asset. You want as few of them as you can get away with, without compromising the actual asset: the functionality.

> A huge part of the job of Software Engineering is producing the right amount of code at the right time.

Absolutely true, however my experience says that the correlation between "good software engineering practices" and "positive business outcomes" is, at best, small.

120 kloc mostly from one single developer copy-pasting and keeping non-compilable code for an obsolete target "for reference" for a decade, becoming both a ball of mud and a whole pantheon of god classes? No unit tests, no code review? Won awards.

Properly engineered, mandatory code review, mandatory unit tests, dev meetings to knowledge-share? People with the money said too slow, closed it down.

(Sometimes people bring up how bad Musk's code was at PayPal. I never bothered investigating. Successful product though, wasn't it?)
children.textZhHtml:
> 代码行是一种负担，而不是一种资产。您希望尽可能少地使用它们，而不影响实际资产：功能。

>软件工程工作的很大一部分是在正确的时间生成正确数量的代码。

绝对正确，但是我的经验表明，“良好的软件工程实践”和“积极的业务成果”之间的相关性充其量很小。

120 kloc 大部分来自单个开发人员复制粘贴和为过时的目标保留不可编译的代码“以供参考”十年，成为一团泥球和一整套神类？没有单元测试，没有代码审查？荣获奖项。

正确设计、强制代码审查、强制单元测试、开发会议以分享知识？有钱的人说太慢了，把它关掉了。

（有时人们会提到马斯克在 PayPal 的代码有多糟糕。我从来没有费心去调查。不过是成功的产品，不是吗？）

children.author: hatefulheart
children.textRawHtml:
Survivorship bias, you don’t know of all the failed projects that couldn’t get off the ground because of incompetent development team and practices that lead a product to its demise, or a product that is possible within constraints that otherwise could have been a success, but not realised by sloppy work and incompetence.

Furthermore the dependencies you choose to build your product are presumably filtered for engineering practices or world class engineers. So given the choice you yourself prefer top quality engineering, so do your customers. Much in the same way you are a customer of your projects dependencies. Difference being, as developers we get to see how the sausage is made, our customers only see second and third order effects.
children.textZhHtml:
幸存者偏差，你不知道所有失败的项目都是因为不称职的开发团队和实践而无法启动，从而导致产品消亡，或者产品在限制条件下可能会成功，但由于草率的工作和无能而未能实现。

此外，您选择构建产品的依赖项可能会针对工程实践或世界一流的工程师进行过滤。因此，如果您自己更喜欢高质量的工程，那么您的客户也是如此。就像您是项目依赖项的客户一样。不同之处在于，作为开发人员，我们可以看到香肠是如何制作的，而我们的客户只能看到二阶和三阶效应。

children.author: ben_w
children.textRawHtml:
> Survivorship bias

The statistical problem is small sample size, not survivorship bias, as I got to see things before failure. These two examples are merely illustrative of things I've seen.
children.textZhHtml:
> 幸存者偏差

统计学问题在于样本量太小，而不是幸存者偏差，因为我在项目失败前就看到了这些问题。这两个例子只是用来佐证我所见过的情况。

children.author: miltava
children.textRawHtml: I think both are at play here and I don’t know about Musk’s programming skills. But it seems that they had other very good programmers (including levchin). So maybe business success can buy good engineers to clean the messed up code. I’m not sure how it goes with AI now though.
children.textZhHtml: 我认为这两方面都在起作用，至于马斯克的编程水平我就不清楚了。但看起来他们还有其他非常优秀的程序员（包括列夫琴）。所以也许商业上的成功能买来优秀的工程师去清理那些乱七八糟的代码。不过我现在不太清楚AI领域情况如何。

children.author: aiisjustanif
children.textRawHtml:
> you don’t know of all the failed projects that couldn’t get off the ground because of incompetent development team and practices that lead a product to its demise

Trying to ignore the nuance is hard in your position or the following one I’ll give is difficult.. but is the opposite potentially true as well? We don’t know how many projects failed because of over optimizing, too much time spent on design and engineering decisions. It’s of getting out and MVP to market. I only say this because I have been apart of a few of these.
children.textZhHtml:
> 你不知道有多少失败的项目因为开发团队无能以及导致产品走向灭亡的糟糕实践而无法启动

站在你的立场很难忽略其中的细微差别，或者我接下来要说的情况也很难理解……但反过来想，情况是否也可能恰恰相反？我们不知道有多少项目因为过度优化、在设计和工程决策上花费太多时间而失败。关键在于尽快将MVP推向市场。我之所以这么说，是因为我曾亲身参与过几个这样的项目。

children.author: resonious
children.textRawHtml: Over optimizing and spending too much time on engineering decisions is also something an incompetent development team would do.
children.textZhHtml: 过度优化并在工程决策上花费过多时间，也是无能开发团队会做的事情。

children.author: bsenftner
children.textRawHtml: Well, that's the other side of incompetence: they know how to spin their tools, but they don't know when to stop, or how to stop the change requests, the balance between shippable, maintainable, and what the market wants at that time.
children.textZhHtml: 嗯，这就是无能的另一面：他们知道如何玩转自己的工具，但不知道何时该停手，也不知道如何遏制需求变更，无法把握可交付性、可维护性与当时市场需求之间的平衡。

children.author: hatefulheart
children.textRawHtml:
I understand and of course I am familiar with the hypothetical you are trying to set up here but I was specifically pointing out a logical fallacy I see banded round all the time by people who should know better or educate themselves.

I will say that if “good engineering practices” comes up in your root cause analysis for a failure to launch a product you are not thinking critically.
children.textZhHtml:
我理解，当然我也很熟悉你在这里试图构建的假设情境，但我特意指出的是一个逻辑谬误，我看到那些本该更懂行或该去自我学习的人却总是把这种谬误挂在嘴边。

我想说的是，如果在产品发布失败的根因分析中出现了“良好的工程实践”这种说辞，那就说明你没有在批判性地思考。

children.author: jmcqk6
children.textRawHtml:
> Absolutely true, however my experience says that the correlation between "good software engineering practices" and "positive business outcomes" is, at best, small.

One of the most uncomfortable truths about our profession is that there is no floor to how bad software can be while still making people billions of dollars.
children.textZhHtml:
> 绝对正确，但我的经验表明，“良好的软件工程实践”和“积极的业务成果”之间的相关性充其量也很小。

关于我们这个行业最令人不安的事实之一是，软件可以有多糟糕，但仍然可以为人们带来数十亿美元。

children.author: 21asdffdsa12
children.textRawHtml: The first thing they buy of the success money though, is a struggling technical competitors, so that this team can clean up there mess. This would mean that AI is only a good contributor at startups and with prototypes.
children.textZhHtml: 他们用成功的钱买的第一件事就是寻找一个苦苦挣扎的技术竞争对手，这样这个团队就可以清理那里的烂摊子。这意味着人工智能只是初创公司和原型的良好贡献者。

children.author: andrewaylett
children.textRawHtml:
There's a trade-off to be made, and it's not necessarily clear where the trade-off sits for any particular company, or even team within the company.

One product person described it as eating vs breathing. Availability is like breathing: if you stop being available (including, but not limited to, because your software is a big ball of mud) then you're going to die pretty quickly. Product is like eating: you might not die so quickly but if no-one's buying what you're selling then you're still not going to survive.

The team I'm part of is a platform team, so we're closer to being lungs than being stomach. We can (and I appreciate being able to) focus more on stability than feature development.
children.textZhHtml:
需要做出权衡，对于任何特定公司，甚至公司内的团队来说，这种权衡并不一定明确。

一位产品人员将其描述为“吃东西”与呼吸。 可用性就像呼吸：如果你不再可用（包括但不限于，因为你的软件是一个大泥球），那么你很快就会死掉。 产品就像吃饭：你可能不会死得那么快，但如果没有人买你卖的东西，那么你仍然无法生存。

我所在的团队是一个平台团队，所以我们更接近于成为肺而不是胃。 我们可以（并且我很高兴能够）更多地关注稳定性而不是功能开发。

children.author: kingds
children.textRawHtml: I'd argue PayPal was a successful business, not a successful piece of software.
children.textZhHtml: 我认为PayPal是一家成功的企业，而不是一款成功的软件。

children.author: ben_w
children.textRawHtml:
That's kind of the point.

The software only had to be good enough to support the business. It doesn't have to win a Turing Award, and probably wouldn't help the business if it did.
children.textZhHtml:
这正是重点所在。

软件只需要足够好以支撑业务运转即可。它不需要去赢得图灵奖，而且就算赢了，可能也对业务没什么帮助。

children.author: nyeah
children.textRawHtml: Lines of code are a business liability. They are future cost.
children.textZhHtml: 代码行数是企业的负担。它们代表着未来的成本。

children.author: preisschild
children.textRawHtml: Good point. In my experience even hobby free software projects are generally better engineered than most proprietary software sold by businesses
children.textZhHtml: 说得好。根据我的经验，即使是业余的免费软件项目，其工程质量通常也优于企业销售的大多数专有软件。

children.author: rusk
children.textRawHtml:
> Successful product though, wasn't it

If you can call being an “also ran” in a field they had a ten year march on their competitors in success, yeah.

Truth be told it was the shoddy code they were forced to use for the vanity of their paymaster might well have held them back, though manifestly that is not a bad thing. Probably the best outcome, really.
children.textZhHtml:
> 不过产品还是挺成功的，不是吗？

如果你能把在一个领先竞争对手十年的领域里沦为“陪跑者”称为成功的话，那确实是。

说实话，他们被迫为了金主的虚荣心而使用的劣质代码很可能拖了他们的后腿，但显然这并非坏事。实际上，这或许是最好的结果。

children.author: DanielHB
children.textRawHtml:
> Lines of code are a liability, not an asset.

I have been saying this for years, I once had a heated argument about a small system of maybe 1000 lines of code that was technically superior and more scalable but was freaking 1000 lines of code to maintain compared to the quick and dirty 10 lines of code it was suppose to abstract and make generic (for future use of course).

That with also countless debates over insignificant features in frontend apps at the cost of extra code. Frontend code is very susceptible to this maintenance cost dilema.

Many developers are too focused on delivery value compared to maintenance cost. It is unfortunate that non-technical management can see value delivered, but not maintenance cost incurred. With LLM-assisted code this has become many times worse.
children.textZhHtml:
> 代码行是一种负担，而不是一种资产。

我多年来一直这么说，我曾经对一个可能有 1000 行代码的小系统进行了激烈的争论，该系统在技术上更优越且更具可扩展性，但与它应该抽象和通用的快速而肮脏的 10 行代码（当然是为了将来使用）相比，要维护的代码却需要 1000 行。

这也引起了无数的争论以额外代码为代价来覆盖前端应用程序中无关紧要的功能。前端代码很容易受到这种维护成本困境的影响。

许多开发人员过于关注交付价值而不是维护成本。不幸的是，非技术管理人员可以看到交付的价值，但看不到产生的维护成本。使用 LLM 辅助代码，情况会变得更糟。
